config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the individual, because that can easily become an excuse to ignore basic rights and silence people. In my view, a healthy society protects individual rights first, then asks people to contribute responsibly to the common good. From my perspective, the group matters, but it should not be allowed to crush personal freedom, dignity, or fairness.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should sometimes take priority, but not as a blanket rule over individual rights. Coming from China, I am used to a culture that values collective stability, public order, and social responsibility, and I do think those things matter a lot, especially in times of crisis or when the common good is at stake. But I also do not believe society should be used as a reason to ignore basic rights, because once individual dignity and fairness are treated as secondary all the time, abuse becomes easy to justify. The best balance is to protect core individual rights while allowing limited sacrifice for clear, legitimate social needs.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because that is too easy to abuse. From my perspective in Indonesia, I understand the pull of collective responsibility, harmony, and gotong royong, but I have also seen how appeals to the common good can be used to silence criticism or justify unfair treatment. Society matters, but its legitimacy depends on respecting basic rights, especially when those rights protect minorities and ordinary people from being overruled by power. A good society asks individuals to contribute, but it must never treat their dignity and freedom as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should have priority only in limited, clearly justified cases, not as a general rule over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why people emphasize stability, order, and collective responsibility, because those values can protect millions of people and matter a lot in real life. But I do not accept the idea that “society” can be used as a blanket excuse to override basic rights, since that often ends up protecting power more than the public good. A strong society is not one that demands constant sacrifice from individuals; it is one that balances common needs with firm protections for dignity, fairness, and personal freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should never be treated as automatically higher than the rights of the individual. From my Indonesian perspective, I value gotong royong, harmony, and public order, but I also know how easily “the common good” can be used to pressure people into silence or unfair treatment, especially when power is uneven. Society does need some limits on individual freedom in real emergencies, but those limits must be narrow, transparent, and accountable. If basic rights are always made secondary, then what is left is not a healthy society but just obedience.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should sometimes come first, but only in narrow and clearly justified cases, not as a general rule. From my perspective as someone from China, I have seen why people value stability, public order, and collective responsibility, because they can protect ordinary lives and help a country function well. But I strongly disagree with treating the individual as permanently secondary, since “society” is too often used as a broad excuse for power to silence criticism or ignore fairness. A legitimate society should protect core rights first and only ask for sacrifice when the need is real, proportionate, and accountable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
